_meta.purpose: 10 personalized press pitches targeting top outlets. Each has a specific journalist + a beat-aligned angle. Send via gmail or Outreach.io. Don't blast — these are individual sends.
_meta.send_sequence: Tue 8 AM ET (your time zone). 1 follow-up after 5 business days. Stop after 2.

pitches.subject: PropertyDNA: first MCP server for real estate intelligence — Decoder angle
pitches.body:
Hi Nilay,

Long-time listener. Loved the recent Decoder on agentic AI distribution.

We just shipped @propertydna/mcp-server — the first Model Context Protocol server for property intelligence. Meaning Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, ChatGPT (via wrapper), and any MCP-aware AI tool can now query property valuations, flood-zone designations, comp analysis, and luxury-provenance dossiers directly from inside the user's AI session.

The Decoder angle I think you'd find interesting: this is one of the first vertical-specific MCP servers shipping with real consumer-facing utility (not just dev infrastructure). It's a case study in what happens when MCP-enabled AI distribution meets a regulatory information-asymmetry market like residential real estate. We index 3.58M parcels across 9 states from county assessor data — no MLS license required. Free for buyers, monetized to enterprises.

Happy to demo live or send a sandbox. Press kit: https://thepropertydna.com/press-kit. Founder background: built this over 18 months self-funded after watching three close friends get steered wrong by their buyer's agents.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA
https://thepropertydna.com

pitches.angle: Florida insurance crisis through buyer data
pitches.subject: 1.4M Florida homes now on Citizens — the binder shock buyers don't see at offer
pitches.body:
Hi Nicole,

Thanks for your recent piece on the Florida insurance market — sharp framing on the depopulation paradox.

We might have a buyer-side data angle that complements it: 1.4M Florida homes are now insured only by Citizens (the state-run insurer of last resort), up from 500K in 2020. The challenge is structural — buyers find out at the binder, not at the offer, because the seller's carrier doesn't transfer.

PropertyDNA — institutional-grade property intel built for retail buyers — surfaces the carrier-tier per zip code, including post-Helene/Milton revised FEMA AE designations, at the offer stage. We can show you the data: how many properties under contract right now will face a 2-3x insurance premium increase at the binder, by metro.

If useful for a follow-up piece on the bind/offer information gap, happy to send the dataset + introduce you to a couple buyers who walked away because of what they saw. Press kit: thepropertydna.com/press-kit.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA

pitches.angle: Robinhood for real estate + asymmetric data thesis
pitches.subject: Robinhood for residential — building the sovereign property index
pitches.body:
Hi Patrick,

Following your work on iBuyer dynamics and the prop-tech consolidation.

We've been building PropertyDNA over the last 18 months — the thesis is straightforward: residential real estate is one of the largest information-asymmetric markets in the country. Every $10K negotiated off the price costs the buyer's agent ~$250 in commission. The buyer carries 100% of the financial risk with 10% of the data.

What we shipped: a sovereign parcel index (3.58M and growing across 9 states) sourced from county assessors directly — no MLS license required. DNA-scored valuations, FEMA flood zone (incl. post-Helene revisions), permit history (BuildZoom + county portals), comparable sales (full ring, not 3-comp CMA). Free for buyers via web + iOS. Monetized through realtor/lender/insurance/enterprise API access.

The market frame: we're building the Robinhood layer for residential real estate — the buyer-facing tool that retail investors actually had with stocks but never had with their largest single asset.

Happy to send the data, the methodology page, or jump on a call if it's a fit.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA
https://thepropertydna.com

pitches.journalist: Jim Dalrymple II / Andrea Brambila (technology editors)
pitches.angle: First MCP for real estate + agent-tool implications
pitches.subject: PropertyDNA shipped the first MCP server for real estate — buyer-side implications for agents
pitches.body:
Hi Jim,

Following your tech coverage at Inman.

Quick news drop that I think is novel for the real estate channel: we published @propertydna/mcp-server on npm yesterday — the first Model Context Protocol server for property intelligence. Meaning Claude / Cursor / ChatGPT can now query DNA-scored valuations, flood zones, permits, comps, off-market matches directly from inside the user's AI session.

Why I think Inman readers should care: buyer-side AI tools are about to fragment the agent's information advantage in real-time. Buyers who use AI for major decisions (already millions) can now run institutional-grade property intelligence from their normal AI workflow. That's a structural shift in how the agent's value proposition gets framed.

We also ship a Realtor Pro tier ($149/mo) with branded reports + listing tools — so this is positive-sum for agents who lead with information rather than gatekeep it.

Happy to demo on a video call or send embed/screenshots. Press kit: thepropertydna.com/press-kit.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA

pitches.subject: PropertyDNA 3.58M-parcel API: lender + insurer integrations now live
pitches.body:
Hi Sarah,

Long-time HousingWire reader, particularly the lending/policy coverage.

We launched PropertyDNA's enterprise API tier this week — 3.58M-parcel intelligence feed across 9 states (AZ, CA, NV, WA, TX, CT, FL, NY) with DNA-scored valuations, FEMA NFHL designations (incl. post-Helene/Milton revisions), permit history, and risk-tier classification.

The lender angle: pre-qualified buyers already running PropertyDNA reports on specific addresses are mid-search by definition. The insurer angle: real-time quote-at-offer capability vs the broken binder-after-offer workflow that's killing FL deals.

If you're covering lender tech consolidation or the insurance market structural shift, our data is available for analysis. Happy to send a sample query or set up an embargoed briefing.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA
partnerships@thepropertydna.com

pitches.subject: Self-funded, free-for-consumers prop-tech with AI-native distribution
pitches.body:
Hi Mary Ann,

Following your prop-tech coverage — particularly the recent Equity podcast segments.

Quick founder pitch I think is on-format: PropertyDNA is 18 months in, fully self-funded, just shipped two things this week — (1) free iOS + web reports across a sovereign 3.58M-parcel index from county data, no MLS license, and (2) the first MCP server for property intelligence (Claude/Cursor/ChatGPT distribution).

The business model: free for consumers, paid Pro tiers for realtors + investors, enterprise API for lenders/insurers/iBuyers. The thesis: information asymmetry in residential real estate is a fixable market dysfunction, and AI-native distribution accelerates the fix.

No raise so far. Happy to share the build metrics + revenue stack if it's useful for a piece on the bootstrapped prop-tech category.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA
https://thepropertydna.com

pitches.angle: Information asymmetry as housing policy lever
pitches.subject: Information asymmetry in residential real estate — a policy angle
pitches.body:
Hi Jerusalem,

Love your work on housing supply + policy framing. Your recent piece on the disclosure-form fiction was particularly sharp.

We've been building PropertyDNA — institutional-grade property intelligence for retail homebuyers — because the information asymmetry between agent + buyer is a fixable market dysfunction the policy tools haven't really addressed. Disclosure forms transfer 1% of relevant data. Asymmetric information transfers 99%.

What we ship that policy can't legislate into existence: 47 data sources joined per property (county assessor + FEMA + USGS + crime + permit history + insurance trajectory), a DNA score with confidence interval, full-ring comp analysis (not the 3-comp CMA the agent picks), free for buyers, on demand.

I think there's a piece worth writing on what happens when retail buyers get the same data access institutional ones do — and what that does to the structural commission economics. Happy to send the data + jump on a call if it's a fit. We have anonymized case studies + accuracy stats live at thepropertydna.com/accuracy.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA

pitches.angle: AI consumer tools moving into vertical markets
pitches.subject: AI is moving into vertical consumer tools — case study from real estate
pitches.body:
Hi Lauren,

Following your work on the AI consumer-tool category — Gadget Lab is a regular listen.

We just shipped what I think is a clean case study for where AI-native consumer tools are heading: PropertyDNA, free property intelligence platform for homebuyers, now distributable inside any MCP-aware AI client (Claude Desktop, Cursor, ChatGPT, etc.) via @propertydna/mcp-server on npm.

The consumer angle: buyers using AI for major decisions can now run DNA-scored property reports inside the chat where they're already asking 'should I buy this house?' That's vertical AI distribution catching up to the horizontal LLM consumer experience.

The broader pattern I think you'd find interesting: this is one of the first non-developer-facing MCP servers shipping with real consumer utility. If you're writing about where AI consumer tools are going beyond chat, happy to demo live and walk you through the architecture.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA
https://thepropertydna.com

pitches.subject: Verified luxury provenance database: William Cody, Lautner, Neutra at-sale verification
pitches.body:
Hi Vivian,

Following your luxury real estate coverage at Forbes.

We've built something I think might be useful for the luxury reporting beat: a verified provenance database for architecturally significant homes. 92 Tier-A verified dossiers across the country (William Cody, John Lautner, Richard Neutra, Frank Lloyd Wright, Philip Johnson Glass House, etc.) cross-referenced against primary-source archives — Architectural Digest back issues, Palm Springs Modernism Committee, county deed records, AIA chapter commission catalogs.

We also flag claims that don't survive verification. The luxury market has a lot of 'Frank Lloyd Wright-inspired' listings that turn out to be 'home built by a guy who once visited Taliesin' — which is its own kind of consumer protection issue.

If you're working on a piece about the verification gap in luxury listings or want access to the verified database for a specific property, happy to help. thepropertydna.com/dossiers shows the public-facing version.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA

pitches.journalist: Stefanos Chen / Debra Kamin (residential real estate)
pitches.subject: Building the Robinhood for residential — sovereign property index
pitches.body:
Hi Stefanos,

Long-time NYT Real Estate reader.

Quick pitch I think fits the section: PropertyDNA is 18 months into building what I'd call the Robinhood for residential — institutional-grade property intelligence for retail homebuyers, free for consumers, sourced from a sovereign 3.58M-parcel index that doesn't require an MLS license.

Why it might be interesting for a piece: we just published our methodology + accuracy dashboard publicly (most competitors don't), and we shipped the first MCP server so the data is queryable directly from inside Claude / ChatGPT / Cursor. Buyer-side data democratization is suddenly happening in a real way, and the structural commission economics of residential real estate haven't priced it in yet.

The story arc that might fit NYT's framing: when the buyer-side of a long-asymmetric market finally gets the data access the sell-side has had for decades, what happens to the negotiation? We have anonymized case studies showing 14% comp-spread cherry-picking, $12K-on-average open-permit findings, and Florida buyers walking away when they see the carrier-tier reality.

Happy to send data + arrange interviews.

Dan Stuart
Founder, PropertyDNA
https://thepropertydna.com
